name: Narrative Scenario / Situational Quiz
core_claim: The Narrative Scenario / Situational Quiz is not a single validated psychometric construct but a DELIVERY FORMAT: short fictional vignettes ('a character faces choice X — what should they do?', or 'what would you do?') that simultaneously elicit (a) a moral-values profile and (b) a narrative-preference signal, while disguising the act of measurement as a story. Its theoretical backbone is Moral Foundations Theory (MFT; Haidt, Graham, Atari), which holds that moral judgement rests on a small set of innate-but-culturally-tuned intuitive foundations — originally five (Care/harm, Fairness, Loyalty/ingroup, Authority, Purity/sanctity) and in the 2023 revision (MFQ-2) six (Care, Equality, Proportionality, Loyalty, Authority, Purity). The methodological backbone is the vignette / situational-judgement-test (SJT) tradition, instantiated in moral psychology by the Moral Foundations Vignettes (MFV; Clifford, Iyengar, Cabeza & Sinnott-Armstrong, 2015), a standardized database of 132 short scenarios each depicting a behaviour that violates one specific foundation. The link to media preference is unusually direct because narrative fiction IS structured moral conflict: Tamborini's Model of Moral Intuition and Media Entertainment (MIME, 2011) argues that the salience and (un)expected upholding/violation of specific moral foundations is a primary driver of enjoyment versus appreciation of a story. A viewer who weights Loyalty and Authority heavily will appraise, root for, and enjoy different characters and resolutions than one who weights Care and Equality — so a viewer's moral-foundation profile is a theory-grounded predictor of which dramatic conflicts, which protagonists (hero vs antihero vs villain), and which thematic resolutions land as satisfying versus alienating. The quiz format additionally harvests a second, parallel signal — narrative-style preference (do you want the morally clean resolution or the ambiguous one; the vengeance or the forgiveness; the institutional loyalty or the whistleblower) — from the SAME items, which is the engagement/efficiency argument for the format.
predictive_validity_for_media: Evidence is moderate, theory-rich but title-level-thin, and concentrated in communication science rather than recommender-systems benchmarks. (1) MFT predicts genre/content preference: the music-lyrics study 'More Than Words: Linking Music Preferences and Moral Values Through Lyrics' (arXiv 2209.01169) showed moral-foundation profiles inferred from listening predict music genre — high Binding (Loyalty/Authority/Purity) foundations associate with country and Christian music; low Binding associates with punk and hip-hop whose lyrics challenge tradition — and that music preference alone could recover moral values, with demographics adding only marginal lift. (2) MFT structures character perception and enjoyment: Eden, Grizzard and colleagues showed audiences perceive narrative characters along the five-factor MFT structure (distinct perceptions of a character's Care/Fairness/Loyalty/Authority/Purity), that heroes are perceived to weight individualizing foundations (Care, Fairness) more than villains, and (Eden et al., 'Repeated Exposure to Narrative Entertainment and the Salience of Moral Intuitions', Journal of Communication 2014) that narrative exposure shifts the momentary salience of specific foundations. (3) Tamborini's MIME and the antihero literature (Krakowiak, Tsay-Vogel; Janicke; Grizzard) link viewer moral profiles and moral-disengagement cues to enjoyment of morally ambiguous protagonists — viewers higher on flexibility/lower on Binding tolerate and enjoy antiheroes and moral greyness more. (4) The enjoyment-versus-appreciation distinction (Oliver & Bartsch; Tamborini): moral-foundation match drives 'enjoyment' (pleasurable, easy, pulls continued viewing) whereas moral challenge drives 'appreciation' (meaningful, taxing). CAVEATS: most effects are at the level of WHICH MORAL CONFLICT / WHICH CHARACTER-TYPE is enjoyed and the thematic/character axis, NOT validated title-level ranking; effect sizes for MFT predicting concrete media choice are modest and frequently confounded with political ideology (the binding/individualizing split tracks left-right) and with the Big Five (Openness correlates with low Binding); and the vignette/SJT FORMAT itself, while well-validated as a measurement device (MFV correlates with the MFQ and discriminates foundations), has not to my knowledge been validated specifically as a cold-start media-recommendation feature. [uncertain]
instrument_design: Two design lineages combine here. (A) The questionnaire backbone: MFQ-30 (30 items, two parts — moral-relevance ratings and agree/disagree judgements, 6 items per foundation across 5 foundations), the MFQ-20 short form (20 items, 4 per foundation), and the current MFQ-2 (Atari et al., 2023: 36 items, 6 items each across the SIX foundations Care, Equality, Proportionality, Loyalty, Authority, Purity; reported completion ~4-5 minutes). (B) The vignette/SJT backbone: the Moral Foundations Vignettes (Clifford et al., 2015) = 132 controlled scenarios, each a one-or-two-sentence behaviour violating a single foundation, rated for wrongness; controlled for syntactic structure and complexity. The Narrative Scenario Quiz fuses these: instead of 'rate how much you agree that respect for authority is a virtue', it presents a STORY ('A soldier is ordered to do something she believes is wrong. She refuses and is court-martialled. Was she right?') and the answer choice reveals the relative weighting of Authority/Loyalty versus Care/Fairness while also capturing a narrative-resolution preference (whistleblower-vindicated vs institution-upheld). MINIMUM VIABLE VERSION for sub-2-minute, 6-10-item onboarding: 6-8 forced-choice or 'who's right?' vignettes, each engineered as a dilemma pitting one foundation cluster against another (e.g. Care vs Authority; Loyalty vs Fairness; Purity/sanctity vs Liberty/autonomy; Equality vs Proportionality), so each item discriminates rather than just measuring one foundation in isolation. Forced-choice between two morally legitimate options is essential — it converts the foundation measurement into an ipsative/relative weighting (which matters more to YOU) and naturally doubles as a narrative-tone probe. This is feasible in ~90 seconds; the trade-off is loss of the validated MFQ-2 scoring and its measurement invariance — the 6-8 disguised dilemmas yield a coarse foundation-emphasis vector, not a clinically scorable MFQ-2 profile.
cold_start_applicability: Good-to-strong on engagement and thematic signal; moderate on signal quality per item; this is one of the more promising formats for HelpME2C specifically. Strengths: (1) zero viewing history required — moral foundations are stable dispositions, so the signal exists before any interaction. (2) The format directly produces TWO orthogonal cold-start signals from one set of items — a moral-foundation emphasis vector (predicts which conflicts/characters/resolutions satisfy) AND an explicit narrative-resolution preference (vengeance vs forgiveness, ambiguity vs clean justice, individual vs institution). (3) High engagement / low survey-feel: a story-and-choice is intrinsically more interesting than a Likert grid, which directly serves completion rate (a North-Star scaffolding proxy is whether onboarding finishes) and reduces the abandonment that kills cold-start data collection. (4) Cross-medium transfer is theoretically clean: moral conflict is medium-agnostic, so a Care-vs-Loyalty weighting predicts preference equally in a Korean revenge thriller, a Japanese found-family anime, or a French institutional drama — arguably better cross-medium than genre tags, which fragment across catalogues. Discounts: (a) each disguised dilemma is a noisy single-item measurement; 6-8 items give a coarse vector, not a precise profile; (b) MFT signal is partially redundant with political ideology and with Openness/Big-Five Binding-vs-individualizing, so incremental value over a values or Big-Five elicitation must be checked; (c) the strongest validated link is to character/conflict appraisal and the enjoyment-vs-appreciation axis, not to title-level ranking, so it must feed a content/theme model rather than rank titles directly; (d) it predicts moral TONE preference, complementary to (not a substitute for) emotional-intensity appetite (Need for Affect) and structural-complexity appetite (Need for Cognition).
group_compatibility_use: This is where the format is most distinctively valuable for HelpME2C, and also where direct evidence is thinnest. Theoretically, a moral-foundation vector is well-suited to two-person aggregation because moral MISMATCH is a strong predictor of co-viewing friction: a story that vindicates vigilante revenge delights a high-Proportionality/low-Care viewer and actively alienates a high-Care/high-Equality viewer, so the dyad's joint satisfaction is dominated by the foundation on which they most diverge. Standard group-recommender aggregation maps on cleanly: (1) AVERAGING the two vectors blends the couple's moral emphasis (good when they are broadly aligned); (2) INTERSECTION favours titles whose central conflict sits on foundations BOTH partners weight similarly (most defensible default — recommend stories where the moral axis is shared, avoid stories that hinge on their point of maximum disagreement); (3) LEAST-MISERY protects against the title that one partner finds morally offensive (e.g. cap content that violates a foundation one partner holds sacred — a Purity-sensitive viewer paired with a transgressive-content lover). Evidence base: there is good general evidence that couples are similar on moral identity and values and that value/personality similarity predicts spouses' life satisfaction (Couple Similarity on Personality, Moral Identity and Spirituality, J. Happiness Studies 2019), but the link from value SIMILARITY to relationship satisfaction is mixed across studies (political-attitude homophily showed only weak satisfaction prediction — RIPS-IRSP 2020), and crucially NO study was located testing moral-foundation aggregation for JOINT MEDIA satisfaction specifically. [uncertain] A defensible starting heuristic: use intersection on the shared-emphasis foundations to select the conflict axis, and apply a least-misery floor on any foundation where the two partners diverge sharply AND one holds it strongly (avoid morally offensive-to-one content), reserving averaging for broadly-aligned dyads. This must be A/B validated, not shipped as established.
quiz_ux_implications: The format's central UX advantage is that it is INHERENTLY disguised — its whole premise is that the situational, story-framed presentation extracts honest moral-weighting answers that a direct 'rate how important loyalty is to you' Likert item would distort. (1) Social-desirability bias is the biggest threat to direct MFT self-report (people over-claim Care/Fairness, under-claim Purity/Authority, and political identity colours answers); the vignette/forced-choice format mitigates this by making BOTH options morally legitimate (a dilemma, not a virtue test), so choosing one does not feel like admitting a vice — this is the core reason to prefer scenarios over the MFQ for a consumer product. (2) Acquiescence bias is largely designed out by forced-choice between two framings (no agree-disagree scale to acquiesce to). (3) Faking is low-stakes in a recommendation context but the format further reduces it because the 'right answer' is non-obvious. (4) Friction is LOW relative to every other instrument surveyed — a story-and-choice reads as content, not as a psychology test, which is the explicit engagement claim. Risks to manage: (a) vignettes must be written so neither option is the transparently 'nicer' choice, or social-desirability creeps back; (b) cultural specificity of scenarios (a scenario about flag-burning or pork-eating carries different moral charge across locales — see cross-cultural field); (c) length discipline — 6-8 vignettes max before it stops feeling fun; (d) the narrative framing should ideally use generic fictional characters, not real-world political hot-buttons, to keep it playful and avoid politicising onboarding. Recommended format: 6-8 single-screen vignettes, each a 1-2 sentence dilemma with 2-3 forced-choice resolutions, each resolution mapped to a foundation-emphasis delta and a narrative-tone tag.
cross_cultural_validity: This is the format's most serious weakness and demands explicit caution for HelpME2C's multilingual catalogue. MFT's five-factor structure does NOT achieve measurement invariance across cultures: Saucier & Iurino's test of the short-form MFQ across 27 countries on five continents found it difficult to specify a five-factor model that converges non-problematically across diverse populations, and the original MFQ was repeatedly criticised as WEIRD-centric, with the Fairness foundation too simplistic for cross-cultural distributive-justice beliefs. The MFQ-2 (Atari et al., 2023) was explicitly built to fix this — validated across multiple cultures (including non-Western samples) and splitting Fairness into Equality and Proportionality precisely because that distinction varies cross-culturally — and has since been validated/replicated in German, Dutch, Turkish, Ghanaian (pre-service teachers), and other contexts with broadly supportive but not uniform results (the binding/individualizing two-factor solution often replicates more cleanly than the full six). The VIGNETTE layer compounds the problem: scenarios are intensely culture-bound — a Purity-violation or Authority-defiance scenario that is morally loaded in one locale is neutral or differently-charged in another, and translation does not fix culturally specific referents. Implications: (a) the abstract foundation vector is more transferable than the specific scenarios; (b) scenarios MUST be localised/re-authored per locale, not merely translated, and ideally use universal-ish dilemmas (family loyalty vs honesty; fairness vs mercy) rather than culture-specific taboos; (c) raw foundation scores are not safely comparable across users from different locales without per-locale calibration; treat the output as a within-user relative emphasis vector, not a cross-user absolute ranking, until invariance is checked for the target languages (Korean, Japanese, Chinese, French, German, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, Hindi, Arabic — only a subset of which have any MFQ-2 validation today).
privacy_consent_implications: Higher sensitivity than most instruments surveyed, with a real GDPR Article 9 exposure. Moral-foundation profiles correlate strongly and reliably with POLITICAL OPINION (the binding/individualizing axis tracks conservative/liberal) and with RELIGIOUS/PHILOSOPHICAL BELIEF (Purity/sanctity loads on religiosity) — both of which are explicitly special-category data under GDPR Article 9. A stored moral-foundation profile is therefore a likely vehicle for inferring protected attributes (political and religious belief), which triggers the explicit-consent requirement and elevated safeguards, and the vignette format can make this worse if scenarios touch real political/religious referents. Practical guidance for HelpME2C: (1) require explicit, specific, purpose-limited consent framed strictly as improving recommendations; (2) do NOT store raw item responses or a labelled psychometric moral profile — store only a coarse derived preference signal (e.g. 'prefers morally clear resolutions', 'tolerates moral ambiguity / antiheroes', 'prefers institution-affirming vs rebellion narratives') decoupled from foundation/political labels; (3) never surface an explicit 'your moral foundations / political-moral profile is X' to the user — it overstates precision and advertises a sensitive inference; (4) prefer generic fictional dilemmas over politically/religiously charged scenarios to reduce the directness of the protected-attribute inference; (5) apply the project's real-deletion guarantees (ADR-0012). This is firmly in stop-and-ask territory per CLAUDE.md (privacy-control flows, special-category data).
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Moral-foundation emphasis is the primary predictor of the MORAL and POWER/SOCIETY axes of the theme vocabulary, and a strong secondary predictor of the relationship and war axes — complementary to and largely orthogonal from Need-for-Affect (emotional intensity) and Need-for-Cognition (structural complexity). Mappings: HIGH CARE/harm-sensitivity -> grief-and-loss, trauma-survival, found-family, war-and-its-toll, civilian-under-occupation, mental-illness-portrayal, loneliness-and-isolation, and AVERSION to gratuitous-cruelty framings; preference for redemption-arc and guilt-and-atonement over vengeance. HIGH EQUALITY (individualizing) -> class-conflict, revolution-and-rebellion, institutional-failure, corruption-and-cover-up, propaganda-and-truth, vigilante-justice (as righteous), underdog-rise, satire-of-power. HIGH PROPORTIONALITY -> vengeance-and-its-cost and vigilante-justice (as deserved retribution), rise-and-fall, heist-and-caper (skilled-earn-their-reward framings), impossible-choice. HIGH LOYALTY (binding) -> found-family read as sacred bond, friendship-tested, betrayal-and-forgiveness, soldier-coming-home, ensemble-mosaic, mentor-and-pupil; aversion to betrayal valorised. HIGH AUTHORITY (binding) -> workplace-procedural and institution-affirming stories, legacy-of-empire, preference for order-restored resolutions; DISCOMFORT with revolution-and-rebellion and whistleblower-vindicated arcs. HIGH PURITY/sanctity (binding) -> folk-horror, cosmic-horror, existential-horror (contamination/desecration dread), meditation-on-death, and aversion to transgressive/toxic-relationship and addiction-and-recovery content framed approvingly. LOW-BINDING / HIGH-FLEXIBILITY profile (the key media tell) -> tolerance and ENJOYMENT of moral-compromise, complicity-with-evil, toxic-relationship, alter-ego, power-corrupts, ethical-dilemma, and the ANTIHERO cluster generally; preference for earned-ambiguity over clean justice. The narrative-resolution signal harvested alongside the foundations maps directly onto texture/structure tags: a preference for unresolved moral ambiguity -> earned-ambiguity, character-study, slow-burn, nonlinear-storytelling; a preference for clear moral resolution -> whodunit, mystery-investigation, redemption-arc, underdog-rise. The single most valuable derived feature is a 'moral-ambiguity tolerance' axis (low-Binding + flexibility) that predicts the antihero/moral-greyness cluster which genre tags capture almost not at all.
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE — as the recommended PRIMARY onboarding format for the moral/thematic axis, and arguably the flagship onboarding interaction given its engagement and dual-signal efficiency. Rationale: it is the only surveyed instrument that (a) directly serves the North Star by maximising onboarding completion (story-and-choice beats Likert grids on engagement, which lifts the completion proxy), (b) yields two orthogonal cold-start signals from one item set (moral-foundation emphasis + narrative-resolution preference), (c) is inherently disguised so it resists the social-desirability bias that cripples direct MFT self-report, and (d) transfers cleanly cross-medium because moral conflict is medium-agnostic. Concrete proposal: 6-8 single-screen fictional dilemmas, forced-choice (2-3 resolutions each), each item engineered to pit one foundation cluster against another AND to expose a narrative-tone preference; place EARLY in onboarding as the engaging hook. Derive and store only a COARSE signal — a moral-ambiguity-tolerance axis plus a small foundation-emphasis vector and a resolution-preference tag — never raw responses or a labelled political/moral profile (Article 9). Feed the output into the theme/content re-ranking model (especially the antihero / moral-greyness / vengeance-vs-forgiveness clusters), NOT into direct title ranking. DEPENDENCIES & FLAGS: (1) requires explicit GDPR Article-9 consent and stop-and-ask review of the privacy flow per CLAUDE.md; (2) scenarios MUST be re-authored per locale (not translated) using broadly universal dilemmas, avoiding political/religious hot-buttons; (3) the two-person aggregation rule (intersection on shared foundations + least-misery floor on sharply-divergent strongly-held foundations) is a hypothesis to A/B test, not an established default; (4) check incremental value over any separate values/Big-Five elicitation to avoid redundancy with the individualizing-vs-binding axis; (5) treat the 6-8-item version as a coarse emphasis vector, not a scorable MFQ-2 profile. Touching the recommendation-engine boundary (packages/ml) for the scoring needs explicit go-ahead per CLAUDE.md.
key_sources: Atari, Haidt, Graham et al. (2023), 'Morality beyond the WEIRD: How the nomological network of morality varies across cultures' / MFQ-2 development & validation, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology (manuscript free): https://psycnet.apa.org/manuscript/2023-99083-001.pdf ; MFQ-2 questionnaires & scoring (free): https://moralfoundations.org/questionnaires/ . Zakharin & Bates (2023), 'Moral Foundations Theory: Validation and replication of the MFQ-2', Personality and Individual Differences / SSRN (SSRN free): https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4384436 ; ScienceDirect (paywalled): https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886923002623 . Clifford, Iyengar, Cabeza & Sinnott-Armstrong (2015), 'Moral foundations vignettes: a standardized stimulus database of scenarios based on moral foundations theory', Behavior Research Methods (FREE PMC): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4780680/ . Saucier & Iurino (2020), 'Testing Measurement Invariance of the Moral Foundations Questionnaire' across 27 countries (FREE PDF): https://pages.uoregon.edu/gsaucier/SaucierIurino2020MoralFoundationsCrossCulturalMeasurementNonInvariance.pdf . Tamborini (2011), 'Moral Intuition and Media Entertainment' (MIME model), Journal of Media Psychology (paywalled; abstract free): https://econtent.hogrefe.com/doi/abs/10.1027/1864-1105/a000031 ; ResearchGate copy: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/251707545_Moral_Intuition_and_Media_Entertainment . Eden et al. (2014), 'Repeated Exposure to Narrative Entertainment and the Salience of Moral Intuitions', Journal of Communication (paywalled): https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/jcom.12098 ; ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/262418134 . 'More Than Words: Linking Music Preferences and Moral Values Through Lyrics' (arXiv, FREE): https://arxiv.org/pdf/2209.01169 . Couple similarity on moral identity & life satisfaction, J. Happiness Studies (2019, paywalled): https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10902-019-00108-8 . Dyadic political-attitude homophily & satisfaction, Int. Review of Social Psychology (2020, FREE): https://rips-irsp.com/articles/10.5334/irsp.1102 . MFQ-2 online demo (free): https://www.idrlabs.com/moral-foundations/test.php .
